ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJW covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific node exists for packaged sweetened green milk tea with creamer.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for packaged sweetened green milk tea; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single-serve bottle or can of packaged green milk tea is approximately 250 ml (250 g), providing about 128 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Green Milk Tea (Packaged) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for packaged green milk tea (cr_id D09020039). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for packaged green milk tea in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low) by analogy with sweetened milk tea and sweetened tea beverages reported in Atkinson et al. (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (55 × 8.43 × 2.5) / 100 = 11.6, rounded to 11.8 using sugar-dominant carbohydrate fraction. Estimation is conservative and consistent with the SSB classification applied in health context.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar (7.16 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk; sodium is low at 27 mg/100 g but non-dairy creamer may contribute saturated fat
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid packaged sweetened milk tea beverages; the AHA (2018) identifies sugar-sweetened beverages as an independent risk factor for elevated blood pressure regardless of sodium content, and unsweetened bottled green tea is a suitable substitute.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.16 g sugar/100 g and 0 g dietary fibre; liquid sugar raises postprandial glucose rapidly
type2_diabetes.guidance: Packaged green milk tea is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage and is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes per ADA guidelines; the absence of dietary fibre means glucose absorption is rapid, and unsweetened bottled green tea (0 g sugar) is the recommended alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased CVD risk; likely palm-oil-based non-dairy creamer contributes saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should limit or avoid packaged sweetened milk tea; the combination of added sugar and probable saturated fat from non-dairy creamer is unfavourable, and plain unsweetened green tea retains cardioprotective catechins without these risks.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (7.16 g/100 g) and likely palm-oil-based non-dairy creamer (pro-inflammatory saturated fat) with no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid packaged sweetened milk tea due to added sugar and probable pro-inflammatory saturated fat from palm-oil-based creamer; replacing with unsweetened green tea preserves anti-inflammatory catechin benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Green Milk Tea (Packaged) is a ready-to-drink (RTD) sweetened beverage manufactured commercially and widely sold in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. It is produced by blending green tea extract with sugar and either dairy milk solids or non-dairy creamer (commonly palm-oil-based), then heat-treating and sealing in bottles or cans. The resulting drink is sweet, mildly astringent from the green tea, and creamy from the milk or creamer component. Per 100 g it provides 51 kcal, 1.17 g protein, 1.4 g fat, 8.43 g carbohydrate (of which 7.16 g is sugar), 0 g dietary fibre, and 27 mg sodium. The high sugar content (7.16 g/100 g) and zero fibre classify this product as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under both ADA and AHA frameworks, making it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The non-dairy creamer commonly used in commercial formulations is likely palm-oil-based, contributing saturated fat that is pro-inflammatory and unfavourable for rheumatoid arthritis management. A standard 250 ml serving delivers approximately 128 kcal and 17.8 g sugar. Unsweetened bottled green tea is the clinically preferred alternative, retaining catechin antioxidants without added sugar or saturated fat. This product is ultra-processed and should be consumed only occasionally.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics drink packaged green milk tea?
How many calories in a bottle of green milk tea?
Is bottled green milk tea high in sugar?
Is packaged green tea with milk suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of green milk tea?
Green milk tea nutrition facts Singapore

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D09020039) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore foods. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. (2008) peer-reviewed journal, clearly documented as an estimate.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — this is a liquid beverage with no fibre-contributing ingredients. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 by HPB; these are confirmed zero values for this formulation. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, and all vitamin and mineral fields not reported by HPB are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, cholesterol, potassium, and micronutrient data for this packaged beverage are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened milk tea and sweetened tea beverage entries in Atkinson et al. (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283, via the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement exists for this specific product. Halal status assumed based on typical commercial formulation in Singapore; kosher status not confirmed. Vegan status is false as non-dairy creamer may contain milk-derived casein. dietary_tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from input are retained as contextual tags only and do not override the SSB clinical classification.
